verses.text: The psalm opens by calling blessed those who walk undefiled in the law of the LORD, seeking Him with the whole heart. The psalmist longs, 'O that my ways were directed to keep thy statutes!' Happiness is found in a life shaped by God's word, and the godly heart yearns to obey it fully.

How shall a young man cleanse his way? 'By taking heed thereto according to thy word.' The psalmist hides God's word in his heart that he might not sin, and delights in its statutes. Scripture stored within is the great guard against sin, treasured more than riches.

'Open thou mine eyes, that I may behold wondrous things out of thy law,' he prays, a stranger on earth longing for God's word. Though princes scorn him, God's testimonies are his counsellors and delight. We need opened eyes to see Scripture's riches, and find in it our truest company.

Cast down so that 'my soul cleaveth unto the dust,' he cries, 'quicken thou me according to thy word.' He asks to be taught and strengthened, ready to 'run the way of thy commandments' once God enlarges his heart. God's word revives the drooping soul and gives feet to run in glad obedience.

'Teach me, O LORD, the way of thy statutes,' he prays, asking understanding to keep the law with his whole heart, and that God incline his heart to His testimonies 'and not to covetousness.' He knows obedience needs God's own teaching and a heart turned by grace from the love of gain.

He prays God's mercies and salvation to come, that he may answer those who reproach him, trusting in God's word. He will speak of God's testimonies even before kings, unashamed, delighting in commandments he loves. The word believed gives both an answer to scoffers and boldness before the great.

'Remember the word unto thy servant, upon which thou hast caused me to hope,' he pleads; God's word has quickened him in affliction. Though the proud deride him, he does not swerve, comforted by God's judgments of old. The promises of God are the believer's hope and song in the night of trouble.

'Thou art my portion, O LORD,' he declares, resolving to keep God's words and seeking His favour with his whole heart. He rises at midnight to give thanks, choosing the company of all who fear God. To have the LORD as our portion is wealth enough, and it makes obedience a glad resolve.

'Thou hast dealt well with thy servant,' he says, confessing, 'Before I was afflicted I went astray: but now have I kept thy word.' He counts it good to have been afflicted, that he might learn God's statutes, prizing the law above thousands of gold. God's chastening turns the wandering heart back to His word.

'Thy hands have made me and fashioned me,' he says; 'give me understanding, that I may learn thy commandments.' He trusts that those who fear God will be glad to see him hope in the word, and asks for tender mercies and a sound heart in God's statutes. The Maker who formed us can also teach us.

'My soul fainteth for thy salvation: but I hope in thy word,' he says, worn 'like a bottle in the smoke,' yet not forgetting God's statutes. Persecuted without cause, he pleads, 'Quicken me after thy lovingkindness.' When the soul is exhausted with waiting, God's word remains its hope and plea for reviving mercy.

'For ever, O LORD, thy word is settled in heaven,' unchanging as His faithfulness through all generations. Had not the law been his delight, he would have perished in affliction; he has seen the end of all earthly perfection, 'but thy commandment is exceeding broad.' God's word is fixed and boundless when all else fades.

'O how love I thy law! it is my meditation all the day.' God's commandments make him wiser than his enemies, teachers, and elders, for he keeps them. 'How sweet are thy words unto my taste! yea, sweeter than honey.' Loving meditation on Scripture yields a wisdom and sweetness the world cannot give.

'Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, and a light unto my path.' Though afflicted and in constant peril, he has sworn to keep God's righteous judgments and will not waver. God's testimonies are his lasting heritage and his heart's joy. Scripture lights the believer's way and is the inheritance he treasures forever.

'Thy word is a lamp unto my feet, and a light unto my path.' Though afflicted and in constant peril, he has sworn to keep God's righteous judgments and will not waver. God's testimonies are his lasting heritage and his heart's joy. Scripture lights the believer's way and is the inheritance he treasures forever.

He hates double-minded vanity but loves God's law, making Him his 'hiding place and my shield: I hope in thy word.' He asks to be upheld that he may be safe and respect God's statutes continually. The believer who loves the word finds in God both a refuge and the strength to stand.

Having done justice, he asks not to be left to his oppressors, longing for God's salvation and word. 'It is time for thee, LORD, to work: for they have made void thy law,' he cries, loving God's commandments 'above gold' and hating every false way. When men cast off God's law, the godly cling to it the more.

'Thy testimonies are wonderful,' he marvels; 'the entrance of thy words giveth light... unto the simple.' He pants for God's commandments and asks to be ordered by the word, freed from sin's dominion. Yet 'rivers of waters run down mine eyes, because they keep not thy law.' Love for Scripture grieves to see it despised.

'Righteous art thou, O LORD, and upright are thy judgments,' commanded in faithfulness. God's word is 'very pure,' and His servant loves it, though small and despised. His righteousness is everlasting and 'thy law is the truth.' The purity and permanence of God's word win a love that trouble cannot cool.

He cries with his whole heart and rises before dawn to hope in God's word, his eyes awake through the night watches to meditate on it. Though the malicious draw near, God is near too. 'Concerning thy testimonies, I have known of old that thou hast founded them for ever.' Earnest, early prayer feeds on the eternal word.

'Consider mine affliction, and deliver me: for I do not forget thy law,' he prays, asking to be revived according to God's word. Though many persecute him, he does not decline from the testimonies. 'Thy word is true from the beginning,' and every righteous judgment endures for ever. God's truth grounds every plea for rescue.

Though princes persecute him without cause, his heart stands in awe of God's word, and he rejoices in it 'as one that findeth great spoil.' He loves the law, praising God seven times a day, for 'great peace have they which love thy law: and nothing shall offend them.' To love and obey God's word is unshakable peace.

He asks his cry to come near and for understanding and deliverance according to God's word, his lips ready to praise. He longs for God's salvation, delighting in the law. He ends humbly: 'I have gone astray like a lost sheep; seek thy servant; for I do not forget thy commandments.' Even the word's lover needs the Shepherd to seek him.
